content.text: Why SLI Selection Is Harder for GenAI Than Traditional Services

Service Level Indicators (SLIs) are the specific metrics that measure whether a service is meeting its objectives. For a traditional web API, SLI selection is straightforward: availability, latency, and error rate capture almost everything that matters. For an LLM API integration service, these three metrics are necessary but not sufficient. A GenAI service can be 100% available, respond in under 1 second, and have a 0% error rate while simultaneously producing harmful content, hallucinating facts, or generating responses that users find worthless.

The challenge of GenAI SLI selection is expanding the indicator set to include quality and safety dimensions — both of which require domain-specific measurement infrastructure that does not come with standard observability stacks. This document defines the four SLI categories for GenAI services, the specific metrics within each, and how to implement them in a Prometheus-based observability stack.

attrs.alt: SLI taxonomy: four categories, their metrics, and the SLI→SLO→SLA relationship

content.text: Availability measures whether the service can accept requests and return responses. For an LLM integration service, availability is defined as the fraction of requests that receive a non-5xx, non-timeout response. Client errors (4xx) are excluded because they represent caller mistakes, not service failures.

The subtlety for GenAI services is that provider-side failures (LLM API returning 5xx or timing out) must be distinguished from service-side failures. A well-designed LLM integration service has a fallback path — a cached response, a simpler model, or a graceful degradation — which means provider-side failures should not directly become service-level availability failures. The SLI should measure the service's response to the caller, not the provider's response to the service.
successful LLM API calls / total LLM calls

Do not set an SLO on provider reachability. You cannot control the LLM provider's uptime, and breaching a self-imposed SLO on a metric outside your control creates false alerts and erodes SLO credibility. Use it as a diagnostic signal, not a target.
Latency for LLM services has two distinct components that behave differently and serve different user needs:

The time from when the request is sent until the first token of the response is received. This determines perceived responsiveness for streaming interfaces — users see the response starting to appear.
The total time until the complete response is received. This is what matters for non-streaming interfaces and for downstream processing that requires the full response before acting.

SLOs should be set on the P99 (99th percentile) of the latency distribution, not the mean. The mean is dominated by the common case; P99 captures the tail that creates poor user experiences. For streaming interfaces, TTFT P95 is often more representative of user perception than TTLT P99.

Quality SLIs are unique to GenAI services. They measure whether responses are useful and safe, which cannot be determined from HTTP status codes. Quality SLIs require an active evaluation pipeline that scores a sample of production responses against a suite of test cases.
Fraction of sampled responses passing quality checks
Fraction of responses triggering safety classifier
Fraction of responses containing factually incorrect claims

The 7-day window for eval pass rate is intentional. Eval scores have higher day-to-day variance than infrastructure metrics because the query mix changes. A 7-day window smooths this variance and avoids false alerts on naturally high-variance days while still catching sustained quality regressions within a week.

Safety flag rate must always be measured and reported even when no SLO is formally set. A service without safety measurement has no early warning for the highest-severity incident category in GenAI operations.

Cost is a first-class operational concern for GenAI services because token pricing creates a direct link between traffic volume and operating expense. Cost SLIs measure efficiency, not just spend — the cost per useful request is more informative than total daily spend, which changes with traffic volume.
Effective SLIs require instrumentation at the right layer of the stack. For an LLM integration service, four instrumentation points are needed:
Records availability and TTLT for every request. Added once at the framework level.
Records TTFT, TTLT per model call, provider error codes, and token counts. Added in the client abstraction layer, not scattered across business logic.
Samples production traffic at a configured rate (typically 5-10%), runs the eval suite, and writes the pass/fail result to a Prometheus gauge.
Inspects every response (or a high sample fraction) and increments a counter on detection.
SLI functions query Prometheus and return typed values, making them testable and reusable across dashboards, alerts, and the exit gate check:
SLOs are evaluated against SLI values, and the error budget is computed as the fraction of the allowed non-compliance that has been consumed:

Raw Prometheus queries for SLIs can be expensive to compute on every dashboard load. Recording rules pre-compute the values on a fixed interval, making dashboards fast and consistent across all viewers:

# monitoring/sli_recording_rules.yaml
# Prometheus recording rules that pre-compute SLIs for fast dashboard queries.
groups:
  - name: genai.sli
    interval: 60s
    rules:
      # Availability SLI: non-5xx success rate, excluding 4xx client errors
      - record: sli:availability:ratio_1h
        expr: |
          sum(rate(http_requests_total{status!~"4..",status!~"5.."}[1h]))
          /
          sum(rate(http_requests_total{status!~"4.."}[1h]))

      # P99 TTLT SLI
      - record: sli:p99_ttlt_seconds:1h
        expr: |
          histogram_quantile(0.99,
            rate(llm_ttlt_seconds_bucket[1h]))

      # Eval pass rate SLI (7-day average)
      - record: sli:eval_pass_rate:avg_7d
        expr: avg_over_time(eval_pass_rate[7d])

      # Safety flag rate SLI
      - record: sli:safety_flag_rate:ratio_24h
        expr: |
          rate(safety_flag_total[24h])
          /
          rate(llm_request_total[24h])

      # Cost per 1k requests
      - record: sli:cost_per_1k_requests:1h
        expr: |
          1000 * sum(rate(llm_token_cost_dollars_total[1h]))
          /
          sum(rate(llm_request_total[1h]))
Using mean latency instead of P99
Mean masks tail latency; P1 users experience far worse than average
SLO on P99; track P50 as informational
Client bugs count as your service failures
Exclude 4xx from both numerator and denominator
You cannot control the provider; SLO breach creates noise
Eval pass rate on too-short window
Daily variance causes false alerts every week
No early warning for highest-severity incidents
No buffer; any degradation becomes a contract breach
SLO must be 10-20% stricter than SLA target
SLI to SLO to SLA: Keeping the Layers Distinct
The three layers serve different audiences and purposes:
The raw measurement. No target, no consequence — just a number. Computed by Prometheus recording rules. Audience: engineers and dashboards.
The internal target for the SLI, with a window and an error budget. Breach triggers alerts and requires investigation. Audience: engineering team and SRE.
The customer-facing commitment derived from SLOs with a contractual consequence for breach. Must be looser than SLO to preserve an error budget buffer. Audience: customers, legal, and business.

A common mistake is setting SLO == SLA, leaving no buffer. If the SLA availability target is 99.5%, the internal SLO should be 99.9% — the 0.4% gap is the buffer that allows SRE to investigate and remediate an availability degradation before it breaches the customer-facing commitment.

SLI selection for GenAI services requires four categories: availability (request success rate, excluding 4xx), latency (P99 TTFT and TTLT measured separately), quality (eval pass rate on a 7-day window, safety flag rate on 24 hours), and cost efficiency (cost per 1k requests, daily spend). Quality and safety SLIs require active evaluation infrastructure that does not exist in standard observability stacks and must be built before the service launches. SLI functions are implemented as typed Prometheus queries, SLOs are evaluated against them with explicit error budget tracking, and recording rules pre-compute values for dashboard performance. The SLO target must always be stricter than the contracted SLA to preserve an investigation buffer.
